It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 3: you have used 169 actions; a strong score needs about 26 or fewer. You are 6.5x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! After clicking the yellow square at row 21, col 38:
- Row 20: 'BBBBYccYBBBBMMMMBBBBcccc'
- Row 21: 'BBBBYYYYBBBBMMMMBBBBcccc'
- Row 22: 'BBBBYYYYBBBBMMMMBBBBcccc'
- Row 23: 'BBBBYYYYBBBBMMMMBBBBcccc'
I see that the right panel has changed! The yellow square area now has 'cc' (charcoal) characters at row 20, cols 38-39. This is where I clicked. It seems like clicking on the yellow square changed some cells to charcoal (the T-shape color).
Wait, let me look more carefully. The yellow square was at rows 20-23, cols 37-40. After clicking at row 21, col 38:
- Row 20: 'BBBBYccYBBBBMMMMBBBBcccc' → Y at col 37, cc at cols 38-39, Y at col 40
- Row 21: 'BBBBYYYYBBBBMMMMBBBBcccc' → YYYY at cols 37-40
- Row 22: 'BBBBYYYYBBBBMMMMBBBBcccc' → YYYY at cols 37-40
- Row 23: 'BBBBYYYYBBBBMMMMBBBBcccc' → YYYY at cols 37-40
So row 20 changed from 'BBBBYYYY' to 'BBBBYccY'. The 'cc' replaced the middle 'YY' of the yellow square. This suggests that clicking on the yellow square "erased" or "colored over" part of it with the T-shape color (charcoal).
This is a major discovery! The right panel objects can be modified by clicking on them. Maybe the goal is to "paint" the right panel objects using the T-shape color, or to erase them.
Let me try clicking on other parts of the right panel to see if I can modify them too.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Major discovery! Clicking on the right panel modifies it.
# Let me check what changed more carefully.

# First, let me see the full right panel state
lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("Right panel after clicking yellow square:")
for r in range(7, 31):
    row_str = lines[r][33:57]
    if 'Y' in row_str or 'M' in row_str or 'c' in row_str:
        print(f"  Row {r}: '{row_str}'")

# Let me try clicking on the magenta bar to see if it changes
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 20, 'col': 46}])

lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("\nAfter clicking magenta bar:")
for r in range(7, 31):
    row_str = lines[r][33:57]
    if 'Y' in row_str or 'M' in row_str or 'c' in row_str:
        print(f"  Row {r}: '{row_str}'")
print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
